title: Acute Cholecystitis & Cholangitis
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Emergency Medicine, Surgery, Internal Medicine, Gastroenterology
objectives: Apply Tokyo Guidelines diagnostic criteria for acute cholecystitis and cholangitis
Grade severity using TG18 classification (I, II, III)
Understand management flowcharts based on severity
Choose empiric antimicrobial therapy per TG18

guidelineReferences.name: Tokyo Guidelines 2018
keyPoints: Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy for Grade I/II
Grade III requires organ support before intervention

vignette.text:
A 58-year-old woman presents with 2 days of worsening RUQ pain, fever, and nausea. Pain radiates to the right scapula and worsens after eating. No jaundice. History of gallstones diagnosed 3 years ago.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, Type 2 DM, Obesity (BMI 34)

Medications: Metformin, Lisinopril

Allergies: Sulfa (rash)

abdomen.palpation: RUQ tenderness with guarding, positive Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest with RUQ palpation)

RUQ_Ultrasound.gallbladder: Distended with wall thickening (5mm), multiple gallstones, positive sonographic Murphy's sign

questions.stem: This patient has RUQ pain, fever, and positive Murphy's sign.
questions.question: What are the Tokyo Guidelines diagnostic criteria for acute cholecystitis?

options.text: Elevated bilirubin required for diagnosis

TG18 DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA: A. LOCAL SIGNS: (1) Murphy's sign, (2) RUQ mass/pain/tenderness; B. SYSTEMIC SIGNS: (1) Fever, (2) Elevated CRP, (3) Elevated WBC; C. IMAGING: Findings characteristic of acute cholecystitis (wall thickening >4mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's); DIAGNOSIS: Suspected = 1 item from A + 1 item from B; Definite = A + B + C confirmed; This patient: Murphy's sign + RUQ tenderness (A) + Fever + Leukocytosis (B) + US findings (C) = DEFINITE acute cholecystitis
CT scan required for all cases
Only imaging is needed for diagnosis

questions.explanation: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 Diagnostic Criteria for Acute Cholecystitis: A. LOCAL SIGNS OF INFLAMMATION: (1) Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest with RUQ palpation), (2) RUQ mass, pain, or tenderness. B. SYSTEMIC SIGNS OF INFLAMMATION: (1) Fever (>38°C/100.4°F), (2) Elevated CRP, (3) Elevated WBC count (>10,000/µL). C. IMAGING FINDINGS: Ultrasound (first-line): Gallbladder wall thickening (>4-5mm), Pericholecystic fluid, Enlarged gallbladder, Sonographic Murphy's sign, Gallstones/sludge. CT/MRI: Similar findings, useful if US inconclusive. DIAGNOSTIC CATEGORIES: Suspected: One item from A + One item from B. Definite: Criteria A + B + C confirmed. This patient: Local: Positive Murphy's, RUQ tenderness ✓. Systemic: Fever 101.5°F, WBC 15,800 ✓. Imaging: Wall thickening 5mm, pericholecystic fluid, stones, sonographic Murphy's ✓. = DEFINITE acute cholecystitis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Cholecystitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to grade this patient's severity to guide management.
questions.question: How is acute cholecystitis severity graded per TG18?

options.text: Severity based only on fever height
All cholecystitis is Grade III
Grading doesn't affect treatment

TG18 SEVERITY GRADING: GRADE I (MILD): No organ dysfunction, mild gallbladder inflammation, safe for cholecystectomy; GRADE II (MODERATE): No organ dysfunction BUT any of: WBC >18,000, Palpable RUQ mass, Duration >72 hours, Marked local inflammation (gangrenous, emphysematous, pericholecystic abscess, biliary peritonitis); GRADE III (SEVERE): Organ dysfunction - CV (hypotension requiring pressors), Neuro (decreased consciousness), Respiratory (PaO2/FiO2 <300), Renal (Cr >2.0), Hepatic (INR >1.5), Hematologic (Plt <100K); This patient: WBC 15.8K, no organ dysfunction, <72h = GRADE I

questions.explanation: TG18 Severity Grading for Acute Cholecystitis: GRADE I (MILD): Does NOT meet Grade II or III criteria. Healthy patient with no organ dysfunction. Mild inflammatory changes in gallbladder. Cholecystectomy is safe and low-risk. GRADE II (MODERATE): No organ dysfunction, but associated with any of: WBC >18,000/µL, Palpable tender mass in RUQ, Duration of symptoms >72 hours, Marked local inflammation: Gangrenous cholecystitis, Pericholecystic abscess, Hepatic abscess, Biliary peritonitis, Emphysematous cholecystitis. GRADE III (SEVERE): Acute cholecystitis with ORGAN DYSFUNCTION: Cardiovascular: Hypotension requiring vasopressors, Neurological: Decreased level of consciousness, Respiratory: PaO2/FiO2 ratio <300, Renal: Creatinine >2.0 mg/dL or oliguria, Hepatic: PT-INR >1.5, Hematological: Platelet count <100,000/µL. This patient: WBC 15,800 (<18K), symptoms 2 days (<72h), no organ dysfunction, no marked local inflammation = GRADE I (MILD).

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Cholecystitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient has Grade I acute cholecystitis.
questions.question: What is the management approach for Grade I cholecystitis?

options.text: GRADE I MANAGEMENT: EARLY LAPAROSCOPIC CHOLECYSTECTOMY preferred (within 72 hours of symptom onset, ideally <7 days of admission); Laparoscopic preferred over open (fewer complications, shorter stay); Antibiotics: Preoperative prophylaxis, NO post-op antibiotics needed for uncomplicated Grade I; If early surgery not feasible: Antibiotics + elective cholecystectomy later; GRADE II: Early cholecystectomy if experienced surgeon available; Otherwise antibiotics + gallbladder drainage (PTGBD) → delayed cholecystectomy; GRADE III: Organ support FIRST, urgent biliary drainage, delayed cholecystectomy after stabilization
All patients need 2 weeks antibiotics before surgery
Grade I always managed medically without surgery
Open surgery preferred over laparoscopic

questions.explanation: TG18 Management by Grade: GRADE I (MILD) - This patient: EARLY LAPAROSCOPIC CHOLECYSTECTOMY: Preferred approach. Timing: Within 72 hours of symptom onset optimal (less inflammation). Laparoscopic > Open (lower wound infection, shorter LOS, faster recovery). Antibiotics: Single preoperative dose (prophylaxis). Post-op antibiotics: Generally NOT needed for uncomplicated Grade I. GRADE II (MODERATE): If experienced surgeon/facility: Early cholecystectomy still possible. If difficult surgery anticipated: Antibiotics + Percutaneous gallbladder drainage (PTGBD), Delayed cholecystectomy once inflammation resolved. Bail-out procedures if intraoperative difficulty (subtotal cholecystectomy, conversion to open). GRADE III (SEVERE): Priority: Organ support and resuscitation. Urgent biliary drainage (PTGBD) to control sepsis. Cholecystectomy DELAYED until patient stabilized. May require ICU care. GENERAL PRINCIPLES: Laparoscopic approach preferred when possible. Transfer to higher-level facility if needed.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Cholecystitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A different patient presents with RUQ pain, fever, and jaundice.
questions.question: How is acute cholangitis diagnosed and differentiated from cholecystitis?

options.text: Cholangitis and cholecystitis are identical
Jaundice is never present in cholangitis

CHARCOT'S TRIAD (50-70%): Fever/chills + RUQ pain + Jaundice; REYNOLDS' PENTAD (severe): Charcot's triad + Altered mental status + Hypotension; TG18 DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA: A. Systemic inflammation (fever, elevated WBC/CRP); B. Cholestasis (jaundice, abnormal LFTs); C. Imaging (biliary dilation, evidence of etiology - stone, stricture, stent); KEY DIFFERENCE from cholecystitis: CHOLANGITIS = Infection of BILE DUCTS (obstruction is key), CHOLECYSTITIS = Infection of GALLBLADDER; Cholangitis requires BILIARY DRAINAGE (ERCP) as definitive treatment
No imaging needed for cholangitis

questions.explanation: Acute Cholangitis - Distinct from Cholecystitis: CLASSIC PRESENTATIONS: Charcot's Triad (present in 50-70%): Fever/rigors/chills, RUQ pain, Jaundice. Reynolds' Pentad (severe cholangitis): Charcot's triad PLUS Altered mental status (confusion), Hypotension/shock. TG18 DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA FOR CHOLANGITIS: A. Systemic inflammation: Fever (>38°C) and/or Elevated WBC/CRP. B. Cholestasis: Jaundice (bilirubin ≥2 mg/dL), Abnormal LFTs (ALP, GGT, AST, ALT >1.5x normal). C. Imaging: Biliary dilation AND/OR Evidence of etiology (CBD stone, stricture, stent occlusion). DEFINITE: A + B + C. SUSPECTED: A + B or A + C. KEY DIFFERENCES: CHOLANGITIS: Infection of bile DUCTS, Requires biliary OBSTRUCTION, Definitive treatment = BILIARY DRAINAGE (ERCP), Cholecystectomy not primary treatment. CHOLECYSTITIS: Infection of GALLBLADDER, Usually from stone impaction in cystic duct, Definitive treatment = CHOLECYSTECTOMY.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Cholecystitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient with cholangitis has fever, jaundice, and hypotension.
questions.question: How is acute cholangitis managed?

options.text: MANAGEMENT PRINCIPLES: (1) Resuscitation: IV fluids, correct coagulopathy, vasopressors if needed; (2) IV Antibiotics: Empiric coverage (see antimicrobial section); (3) BILIARY DRAINAGE - Essential definitive treatment: ERCP preferred (therapeutic - stone removal, stenting), Percutaneous transhepatic (PTC) if ERCP fails/unavailable, EUS-guided drainage as alternative; TIMING by severity: Grade III (severe): Urgent drainage within 24 hours, Grade II (moderate): Early drainage within 24-48 hours, Grade I (mild): Drainage can be elective if antibiotics control infection; Cholecystectomy NOT primary treatment for cholangitis
Emergency cholecystectomy is first-line
Biliary drainage not needed
Antibiotics alone without drainage

questions.explanation: Acute Cholangitis Management: INITIAL MANAGEMENT: Resuscitation: IV fluids (sepsis likely), Correct electrolyte abnormalities, Vasopressors if hypotensive (septic shock), Correct coagulopathy if present (INR elevation common). Antibiotics: Start empiric antibiotics immediately (see next question). Blood cultures before antibiotics if possible. BILIARY DRAINAGE - ESSENTIAL: The KEY intervention - antibiotics alone insufficient. Methods (in order of preference): ERCP: Gold standard, allows therapeutic intervention (stone extraction, sphincterotomy, stenting). Percutaneous Transhepatic Cholangiography (PTC): If ERCP fails or not available. EUS-guided drainage: Emerging alternative. TIMING BY TG18 SEVERITY: Grade III (Severe - organ dysfunction): URGENT drainage within 24 hours, May require ICU care. Grade II (Moderate): Early drainage within 24-48 hours. Grade I (Mild): Elective drainage may be appropriate if antibiotics control infection. CHOLECYSTECTOMY: NOT primary treatment for cholangitis. May be needed later if gallstones are the cause.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Cholecystitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to select empiric antibiotics.
questions.question: What is the antimicrobial approach per TG18?

options.text: COMMON PATHOGENS: Gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterobacter), Enterococcus, Anaerobes (Bacteroides - especially severe/complicated); COMMUNITY-ACQUIRED Grade I-II: Cephalosporin (ceftriaxone, cefazolin) ± metronidazole, Fluoroquinolone (if beta-lactam allergy) + metronidazole, Ampicillin-sulbactam; SEVERE (Grade III) / Healthcare-associated: Piperacillin-tazobactam, Carbapenems (imipenem, meropenem), Cefepime + metronidazole; DURATION: Grade I/II with cholecystectomy - preoperative only (no post-op abx needed); Cholangitis - until source controlled + clinical improvement (typically 4-7 days)
Only gram-positive coverage needed
Anaerobic coverage never needed
All patients need 14 days of antibiotics

questions.explanation: TG18 Antimicrobial Therapy: COMMON PATHOGENS: Gram-negative Enterobacteriaceae: E. coli (most common), Klebsiella spp., Enterobacter spp. Gram-positives: Enterococcus spp. (especially healthcare-associated). Anaerobes: Bacteroides fragilis (more common in severe, complicated, elderly). EMPIRIC REGIMENS: COMMUNITY-ACQUIRED, GRADE I-II: Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily ± Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h. Cefazolin 2g IV q8h + Metronidazole. Ampicillin-sulbactam 3g IV q6h. Penicillin allergy: Fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin 750mg or ciprofloxacin 400mg) + Metronidazole. GRADE III / HEALTHCARE-ASSOCIATED: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h. Meropenem 1g IV q8h (if ESBL concern). Cefepime 2g IV q8h + Metronidazole. DURATION: CHOLECYSTITIS with cholecystectomy: Grade I/II: Preoperative prophylaxis ONLY - no post-op antibiotics needed (per TG18 RCTs). CHOLANGITIS: Continue until source controlled (drainage) + clinical improvement. Typically 4-7 days total.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Cholecystitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You need to recognize severe forms of cholecystitis.
questions.question: What are the complications and severe forms of acute cholecystitis?

options.text: Complications are rare and not important
Gangrenous cholecystitis has better prognosis

COMPLICATED CHOLECYSTITIS: GANGRENOUS: Gallbladder wall necrosis, higher perforation risk, may lack typical signs in elderly/diabetics; EMPHYSEMATOUS: Gas in gallbladder wall (seen on CT/X-ray), associated with diabetes, Clostridial infection, high mortality; PERFORATION: Localized (pericholecystic abscess) or Free (biliary peritonitis - surgical emergency); GALLSTONE ILEUS: Fistula to bowel, stone causes SBO (classic: air in biliary tree + SBO); MIRIZZI SYNDROME: Large stone in cystic duct compresses CBD → jaundice; All require urgent intervention
All complications managed medically

questions.explanation: Complicated Cholecystitis: GANGRENOUS CHOLECYSTITIS: Gallbladder wall necrosis and devitalization. Higher risk of perforation. May have ABSENT Murphy's sign (denervation). Higher risk in: Elderly, Diabetics, Males, Delayed presentation. Requires urgent cholecystectomy. EMPHYSEMATOUS CHOLECYSTITIS: Gas-forming organisms (Clostridium, E. coli). Gas in gallbladder wall on imaging (CT, X-ray). Risk factors: Diabetes (most common), Males, Vascular disease. High mortality (~15-25%). Urgent cholecystectomy required. PERFORATION: Localized: Pericholecystic abscess (walled-off). Free: Biliary peritonitis → surgical emergency. GALLSTONE ILEUS: Chronic cholecystitis → fistula to duodenum/colon. Large stone passes → mechanical small bowel obstruction (SBO). Rigler's triad: Pneumobilia (air in biliary tree), SBO, Ectopic gallstone. Treatment: Surgery to relieve obstruction ± cholecystectomy/fistula repair. MIRIZZI SYNDROME: Large stone impacted in cystic duct or Hartmann's pouch. Extrinsic compression of common bile duct → obstructive jaundice.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Cholecystitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This 58-year-old woman has Grade I acute cholecystitis.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Complete approach: (1) DIAGNOSIS: Confirmed acute cholecystitis - Murphy's sign, RUQ tenderness (A) + Fever, leukocytosis (B) + US findings (C); (2) SEVERITY: Grade I - WBC <18K, <72h symptoms, no organ dysfunction; (3) INITIAL MANAGEMENT: NPO, IV fluids, analgesia, preoperative antibiotics (ceftriaxone + metronidazole given sulfa allergy); (4) DEFINITIVE TREATMENT: EARLY LAPAROSCOPIC CHOLECYSTECTOMY (within 72h of symptoms optimal); (5) POST-OP: No antibiotics needed for uncomplicated Grade I; Advance diet, discharge when tolerating PO; (6) Follow-up: Routine post-op care
2 weeks IV antibiotics before considering surgery
ERCP first then cholecystectomy
Percutaneous drainage as primary treatment

questions.explanation: Complete Management for this patient: STEP 1 - CONFIRM DIAGNOSIS: TG18 criteria met: A (Murphy's, RUQ tenderness) + B (Fever, WBC 15.8K) + C (US: wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's) = DEFINITE acute cholecystitis. STEP 2 - GRADE SEVERITY: Grade I (Mild): WBC 15,800 (<18K), Duration 2 days (<72h), No organ dysfunction, No marked local inflammation. STEP 3 - INITIAL MANAGEMENT: NPO, IV fluid resuscitation, Analgesia (NSAIDs can help prevent progression), Preoperative antibiotics: Given sulfa allergy → Ceftriaxone 2g IV + Metronidazole 500mg IV (not fluoroquinolone if can use beta-lactam). STEP 4 - DEFINITIVE TREATMENT: EARLY LAPAROSCOPIC CHOLECYSTECTOMY: Optimal within 72 hours of symptom onset. Laparoscopic approach preferred. Send gallbladder for pathology. STEP 5 - POST-OPERATIVE: Grade I uncomplicated → NO post-operative antibiotics needed (per TG18 evidence). Advance diet as tolerated. Discharge typically within 24-48 hours. STEP 6 - FOLLOW-UP: Routine surgical follow-up. Counsel on low-fat diet initially.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Cholecystitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected cholecystitis cholangitis?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in cholecystitis cholangitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of cholecystitis cholangitis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing cholecystitis cholangitis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
